transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Quinn. I’m glad you’re here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Hi, thanks for having me. I guess I’ve been thinking a lot about how I manage my time, especially when it comes to social situations. I often find myself feeling anxious when I have friends over, like I’m not doing enough or I’m keeping them waiting. It’s just... I don’t want to disappoint anyone, you know? But it seems like I end up stressing myself out instead.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re feeling a lot of pressure when it comes to social situations, especially with your friends. You want to make sure they’re happy and not disappointed, but that concern seems to lead to anxiety and stress for you. Can you tell me more about what specifically makes you feel anxious in those moments?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it’s just this nagging thought that I’m being rude if I keep them waiting. I feel like I should be more organized and on top of things. When I’m late, I worry they might think I don’t care or that I’m not a good friend. It’s like I’m constantly trying to tweak everything to make it perfect, but then I end up running late anyway. It’s frustrating because I want to enjoy the time with them, but I get so caught up in these thoughts.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It seems like you have a strong desire to be a good friend and to create a perfect experience for your friends, which is really admirable. But that pressure to be organized and avoid being perceived as rude seems to create a cycle of stress for you, leading to the very thing you’re trying to avoid—being late. It must be frustrating to feel like you’re caught in that loop. How do you usually cope with those feelings when they arise?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: It is frustrating, definitely. When I start feeling that pressure, I tend to just... avoid leaving work on time. I think if I can just make things a little better or more polished, it’ll be worth it. But then I end up rushing and feeling guilty for keeping my friends waiting. It’s like I’m stuck in this pattern where I want everything to be perfect, but I know it’s not really working out that way. I guess I just keep hoping that if I try harder, I’ll figure it out.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re caught in a cycle where the desire for perfection leads to avoidance and ultimately more stress. You’re hoping that by putting in extra effort, things will turn out better, but it often results in feeling rushed and guilty instead. It’s understandable to want everything to go smoothly, but it seems like this approach is leaving you feeling stuck. What do you think might happen if you allowed yourself to let go of that need for perfection in those situations?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: That’s a tough question. I think if I let go of the need for everything to be perfect, I might feel a bit more relaxed, but there’s also this fear that things wouldn’t go well. What if my friends didn’t enjoy themselves, or what if I forgot something important? It’s hard to imagine just letting things unfold without trying to control every detail. I guess part of me worries that if I don’t try my hardest, I might disappoint them, and that thought is really uncomfortable.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like there’s a real tension between wanting to feel relaxed and the fear of disappointing your friends. That fear of things not going well or forgetting something important seems to weigh heavily on you. It’s understandable to want to control the situation to ensure everyone has a good time, but it also sounds like that control comes at a cost to your own enjoyment and peace of mind. What do you think it would take for you to feel a bit more comfortable with the idea of letting things unfold naturally?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it would take some practice and maybe a shift in how I view those situations. If I could remind myself that my friends are there to enjoy spending time together, not just to judge how well I’ve organized everything, that might help. Maybe starting small could work—like allowing myself to be a little less prepared for a casual get-together and see how it goes. But it’s still a bit scary to think about. I guess I’d need to build some trust in the idea that it’s okay to not have everything perfect.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a thoughtful approach, Quinn. Recognizing that your friends value your company over the details is a significant insight. Starting small and gradually allowing yourself to be less prepared could be a good way to build that trust in the process. It’s completely natural to feel scared about stepping out of your comfort zone, especially when it comes to something that feels so important to you. What do you think might help you feel more supported as you begin to practice this shift in perspective?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think having open conversations with my friends about how I feel could help. If they know I struggle with this pressure, maybe they’d be more understanding if things don’t go perfectly. Also, having a friend who can remind me to relax or even just share their own experiences could make me feel less alone in it. It might help to know that I’m not the only one who feels this way. Just having that support might make it easier to take those small steps.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a really constructive idea, Quinn. Opening up to your friends about your feelings could create a supportive environment where you can all share your experiences. It’s great that you recognize the value of connection and understanding in easing that pressure. Having a friend who can remind you to relax or share their own struggles could definitely help you feel less isolated in this. How do you think you might start that conversation with your friends?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I could start by just being honest about how I feel when we have gatherings. Maybe I could say something like, “I really want to enjoy our time together, but I often feel a lot of pressure to make everything perfect.” It might help to frame it as something I’m working on rather than just a complaint. I could ask if they’ve ever felt similar pressures, too. I guess I’m a bit nervous about how they’ll react, but I hope they’ll understand and maybe even share their own experiences. It feels like a step in the right direction.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a thoughtful and honest way to approach the conversation. Framing it as something you’re working on invites understanding and opens the door for your friends to share their own experiences, which could foster a deeper connection. It’s completely normal to feel nervous about how they might react, but it seems like you’re taking a brave step toward vulnerability. How do you think you might manage those nerves when the time comes to have that conversation?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I could try to remind myself that it’s okay to be vulnerable and that my friends care about me. Maybe practicing what I want to say beforehand could help, so I feel a bit more prepared. I could also take a few deep breaths before the conversation to calm my nerves. It might help to focus on the positive outcome I hope for—like feeling more connected and supported—rather than getting caught up in the fear of how they might react. Just taking it one step at a time could make it feel less overwhelming.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those are really constructive strategies, Quinn. Reminding yourself of your friends' care and practicing what you want to say can definitely help ease some of that nervousness. Focusing on the positive outcome you hope for is a great way to shift your mindset and make the conversation feel more manageable. Taking it one step at a time is wise, too. It sounds like you’re building a solid plan to approach this in a way that feels right for you. How are you feeling about taking these steps now that we’ve talked about it?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a bit more hopeful, actually. Talking it through has helped me see that it’s possible to approach this in a way that feels manageable. I still have some nerves, but I think I’m starting to feel more confident about opening up to my friends. Just knowing that I have a plan and some strategies makes it feel less daunting. I guess I’m realizing that it’s okay to not have everything figured out right away, and that’s a relief.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: I’m really glad to hear that you’re feeling more hopeful and confident, Quinn. It sounds like this conversation has helped you clarify your thoughts and create a plan that feels manageable. Recognizing that it’s okay not to have everything figured out right away is an important realization, and it can take a lot of pressure off. As you prepare to take these steps, remember to be gentle with yourself. Is there anything else on your mind that you’d like to explore or discuss today?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you, I appreciate that. I think I’ve covered a lot of what’s been on my mind, but I guess I’m still a bit concerned about how to maintain that mindset once I start having those conversations. I don’t want to slip back into old habits of feeling overwhelmed or pressured. Maybe discussing some ways to keep that positive perspective would be helpful?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
It’s completely understandable to want to maintain that positive mindset as you move forward. It can be challenging to break old habits, especially when you’re feeling pressure. One approach might be to regularly remind yourself of the insights you’ve gained from our conversation—like the importance of connection and the understanding that your friends care about you. 

You could also consider setting small, achievable goals for yourself after each gathering, reflecting on what went well and what felt good. Journaling about your experiences might help reinforce that positive perspective, too. What do you think would resonate with you as a way to keep that mindset alive?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Those are great suggestions. I like the idea of journaling about my experiences—it could help me process my feelings and reflect on what went well. I think setting small goals after each gathering could also keep me focused on the positives rather than getting caught up in any mistakes. Maybe I could even create a little reminder for myself, like a note with key insights from our conversation, to look at before I head into social situations. That way, I have something tangible to ground me. I feel like having those tools could really help me stay on track.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
Those sound like wonderful tools to help you maintain that positive perspective! Journaling can be a powerful way to process your feelings and celebrate your successes, no matter how small. Setting small goals and having a reminder note with key insights can serve as great anchors for you in those moments of pressure. It’s clear that you’re thinking thoughtfully about how to support yourself, which is a positive step in itself. 

As you prepare to implement these tools, how do you feel about the idea of checking in with yourself regularly to see how you’re doing with this new approach?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think checking in with myself regularly sounds really helpful. It would give me a chance to reflect on how I’m feeling and whether I’m sticking to my goals. I could set aside a few minutes after each gathering to think about what went well and what I might want to adjust next time. It might also help me recognize any patterns in my thoughts or feelings, so I can address them before they become overwhelming. Overall, I feel like this could be a good way to stay accountable to myself and keep moving forward.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
That sounds like a fantastic plan, Quinn. Setting aside time to reflect after each gathering can provide valuable insights and help you stay accountable to yourself. Recognizing patterns in your thoughts and feelings will empower you to address them proactively, which is a great way to prevent overwhelm. It seems like you’re building a strong foundation for navigating these social situations with more ease and confidence. 

As you move forward with this approach, how do you feel about the support you have around you? Do you think there are ways to strengthen that support as you work on these goals?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel like I have a decent support system with my friends, but I think I could strengthen it by being more open about what I’m working on. If I share my goals and the strategies I’m trying, I believe they’d be more likely to support me and maybe even share their own experiences. It could create a more open dialogue where we can all help each other out. I might also consider reaching out to a couple of friends who I know have faced similar pressures, just to see if they’d be willing to check in with me or share tips. Strengthening that support could make a big difference as I work on these goals.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
It sounds like you have a thoughtful approach to strengthening your support system, Quinn. Being open about your goals and the strategies you’re trying can foster a deeper connection with your friends and encourage them to share their own experiences. It’s great that you’re considering reaching out to those who have faced similar pressures; that could create a sense of camaraderie and mutual support. 

Creating that open dialogue can really enhance your journey and help you feel less alone in this process. As you think about taking these steps, how do you feel about the balance between seeking support and also giving yourself space to navigate this on your own?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content:
That’s a good point. I think finding that balance will be important for me. I definitely value the support from my friends, but I also want to make sure I’m not overly reliant on them. I think it’s essential for me to have my own space to process my feelings and experiences, especially since this is something I want to work on personally. 

Maybe I could set aside time for both—like having a regular check-in with friends while also dedicating time for my own reflection and journaling. That way, I can benefit from their support while still honoring my own journey. It feels like a healthy way to approach it, allowing me to grow both with their help and independently.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
That sounds like a very balanced and healthy approach, Quinn. By setting aside time for both check-ins with your friends and your own personal reflection, you’re creating a supportive framework that honors your individual journey while also allowing for connection. It’s great that you recognize the importance of both aspects—having that space for personal processing and also leaning on your friends for support when you need it.

This dual approach can help you grow in a way that feels authentic and empowering. As you move forward with this plan, how do you feel about the progress you’ve made in our conversation today?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel really positive about the progress we’ve made today. Talking through my thoughts and feelings has helped me clarify what I want to work on and how I can approach it. I appreciate the strategies we discussed, like journaling and checking in with myself, as well as the idea of being more open with my friends. It feels like I have a clearer path forward now, and I’m feeling more hopeful about navigating social situations without that overwhelming pressure. Overall, I feel like I’m leaving this conversation with a better sense of direction and support. Thank you for guiding me through it!
